module.title: EQ & Frequency
module.description: Go beyond basic EQ moves — understand the frequency spectrum deeply, fix problem frequencies surgically, shape tonal character, separate competing instruments, and use mid-side and dynamic EQ techniques.

lessons.title: How Frequency Works in a Mix
lessons.body:
EQ decisions made without understanding the frequency spectrum are guesswork. Once you internalise what lives where — which instruments own which ranges, where mud collects, where clarity lives — every EQ move becomes intentional. This lesson maps the spectrum and explains why mixes get cluttered.

## The Audible Spectrum

Human hearing spans roughly 20 Hz to 20 kHz — about ten octaves. Each octave doubles in frequency. The middle of the piano keyboard sits at A4 = 440 Hz. The deepest bass notes a subwoofer produces sit around 20–40 Hz. The shimmer of a cymbal extends past 10 kHz.

Producers divide this range into bands with distinct sonic characters:

## The Frequency Bands

**Sub Bass — 20 to 60 Hz**
Felt more than heard. The fundamental of a well-tuned 808 sits around 40–50 Hz. Kick drum sub energy is in this range. On small speakers these frequencies are inaudible; on subwoofers they provide the physical impact of a track.

*What lives here*: 808 fundamentals, kick sub, synthesised bass fundamentals in certain genres.
*Problem*: Too much energy here makes a mix sound heavy on big speakers and completely empty on small ones. Ruthlessly high-pass everything that does not need sub energy.

**Bass — 60 to 250 Hz**
Warmth, body, and weight. The fundamental notes of a bass guitar or bass synth live here. The kick drum body (the thud you hear, not just the sub) is in this range. This is also where most low-end mud accumulates in dense mixes.

*What lives here*: Bass/808 body, kick body, low piano notes, cello fundamentals.
*Problem*: Multiple instruments stacking low-end creates a blurry, indistinct bottom — the primary cause of muddy mixes.

**Low Mids — 250 to 500 Hz**
The muddiness zone. Almost every instrument has some energy here, and it piles up fast. Guitars and pianos have boxy resonances here. Drum rooms accumulate here. Excessive low-mid energy makes a mix sound thick and dark — not warm, just congested.

*What lives here*: Lower harmonics of almost all instruments.
*Problem*: The most common source of mix mud. Small cuts here (2–4 dB) on multiple tracks make the entire mix clearer.

**Mids — 500 Hz to 2 kHz**
Presence, body, and intelligibility. The human voice is centred here. Guitar and synth fundamental harmonics are prominent in this range. This is where most musical information lives — melody, rhythm, and harmony are all dominated by the mid frequencies.

*What lives here*: Vocals, guitars, synth harmonics, snare body, piano mid-range.
*Problem*: Harshness and nasality if over-boosted. A mix that sounds thin and lacking body is usually missing energy in the upper portion of this range.

**Upper Mids — 2 to 6 kHz**
Clarity, attack, and edge. This range determines how forward and present an element sounds. The click of a kick drum, the snap of a snare, the consonants of speech — all defined here. This is also the harshness zone: boosting without care creates ear fatigue.

*What lives here*: Transient attacks of drums, vocal consonants, guitar pick attack, synth bite.
*Problem*: Boosting 2–4 kHz aggressively sounds exciting for 10 minutes, then becomes fatiguing. Subtle boosts; larger cuts.

**Presence — 4 to 10 kHz**
Definition, sibilance, and cut-through. This is where lead elements separate from the mix. A presence boost makes a vocal or lead synth cut through without raising its volume. It is also where sibilance (harsh S and T sounds) lives in vocals.

*What lives here*: Vocal sibilance, hi-hat body, synth brightness, string harmonics.
*Problem*: Over-boosted presence sounds thin and digital. Sibilance in this range causes fatigue.

**Air — 10 to 20 kHz**
Shimmer, sparkle, openness. High-shelf boosts here add perceived openness and space to a mix. Most content in this range is harmonics and noise rather than fundamental frequencies.

*What lives here*: Cymbal shimmer, room ambience, synthesised air.
*Problem*: Too much boosting here makes a mix sound harsh and brittle, especially on compressed streaming formats.

## Frequency Masking: Why Mixes Get Muddy

Masking occurs when two sounds occupy the same frequency range at similar loudness — the louder one partially or completely obscures the quieter one. The listener perceives both as a blurred, indistinct wash rather than two separate elements.

**Classic masking example**: A bass synth with a prominent fundamental at 100 Hz and a kick drum with its body at 100 Hz. Both are audible in solo. Together, the lower-energy one disappears into the louder one. The solution is not to raise the quieter element — it is to carve EQ space so they occupy slightly different frequency zones.

Masking is frequency-specific: two elements mask each other at the frequencies where they overlap. They can coexist cleanly at frequencies where only one is present. This is the underlying logic behind EQ for separation, covered in Lesson 4.

## Reading the Spectrum Analyser

Parametric EQ 2 includes a real-time spectrum analyser. Enable it by right-clicking the display. Use it to:

- Identify where an instrument's energy is concentrated
- Spot unexpected resonances (narrow peaks that stick above their neighbours)
- Verify that high-pass filters are working (the left side should fall off)
- See how much low-mid energy is accumulating on non-bass tracks

A critical skill: learn to listen for what you see. When you see a spike at 350 Hz on a chord pad and simultaneously hear the boxy quality of that pad, you are building the frequency-to-sound mapping that makes EQ decisions fast and accurate.
keyTakeaways: The low-mids (250–500 Hz) are the primary source of mix mud — almost every instrument has energy here, and small cuts of 2–4 dB on multiple non-bass tracks clear the mix more than any other single move.
Frequency masking is why elements that sound clear in solo disappear in the mix — when two sounds share the same frequency range at similar levels, the louder one obscures the quieter one.

Learn to connect what you see in the spectrum analyser to what you hear: when a boxy quality coincides with a spike at 350 Hz, you are building the ear-training that makes every future EQ decision faster.
lessons.tryThis: Open a project with at least a kick, bass, and chord pad. Solo the chord pad and enable the Parametric EQ 2 spectrum analyser. Note where most of the energy is — it is likely concentrated between 100 and 500 Hz. Now unsolo and listen to the full mix. Add a high-pass filter at 250 Hz on the chord pad. Compare before and after in the context of the full mix — the pad has not lost its character, but the low end of the mix is suddenly cleaner. This single high-pass cut is one of the highest-impact EQ moves in any dense mix.

lessons.title: Surgical EQ: Fixing Problem Frequencies
lessons.body:
Surgical EQ means making narrow, targeted cuts to remove specific problems — resonances, harshness, muddiness — without affecting the surrounding frequencies. It is the first pass of any EQ session: fix what is wrong before you consider what might sound better.

## Surgical vs Tonal EQ

**Surgical EQ** uses high-Q (narrow) cuts to remove problems. The goal is transparency — the listener should not hear the EQ, only the absence of the problem.

**Tonal EQ** uses low-Q (broad) boosts and cuts to shape the overall character and colour of a sound. This is covered in Lesson 3.

Always do surgical work first. Tonal shaping applied over an unaddressed resonance just makes the resonance sound more coloured.

## The Sweep Technique (Detailed)

The sweep technique locates resonances you can hear but cannot pinpoint:

1. Add a peak band in Parametric EQ 2 with a very high Q (narrow — around 8–12) and a large boost of +12 to +15 dB
2. Solo the track and play it in a loop
3. Drag the frequency slowly across the range where you suspect the problem
4. When the annoying quality becomes grotesquely exaggerated, you have found the resonant frequency
5. Switch the boost to a cut of −4 to −8 dB
6. Reduce the Q to a moderate width (3–5) — the cut should affect the resonance and a little of its neighbours, not a razor-thin slice
7. Adjust the gain until the problem is controlled but the sound still has body

**Important**: Always check the cut in the context of the full mix, not solo. A cut that sounds perfect in solo may be inaudible in the mix — or may have removed something that was contributing to the mix's overall warmth.

## Common Problems and Their Frequencies

**Muddiness / Boominess — 100 to 350 Hz**
Sounds thick, unclear, indistinct. Low-end elements blend together and lose definition. Common on mixes with many instruments stacking low-mid energy.
*Fix*: High-pass non-bass tracks. Add a moderate cut (−2 to −4 dB, Q of 1–2) centred around 200–300 Hz on the most offending elements.

**Boxy / Cardboard — 300 to 600 Hz**
Sounds hollow and enclosed, like audio played in a small box. Common on drum samples recorded in small rooms, cheap keyboards, certain VST presets.
*Fix*: Sweep between 300–600 Hz. Cut 4–6 dB with moderate Q (3–5). Snares often benefit enormously from a cut around 500 Hz.

**Honky / Nasal — 600 Hz to 1.2 kHz**
Sounds like audio played through a phone or a megaphone. Common on guitar amp simulations, certain piano samples, mid-heavy synthesisers.
*Fix*: Sweep between 600 Hz and 1.2 kHz. Narrow cut of 3–6 dB.

**Harsh / Edgy — 2 to 4 kHz**
Sounds aggressive and fatiguing after extended listening. Common on overdriven synthesisers, highly compressed audio, low-quality samples.
*Fix*: A moderate cut at the offending frequency. This range is tricky — too much cutting removes the presence and attack that make elements audible in the mix. Cut conservatively (2–4 dB) and listen carefully.

**Sibilance — 5 to 10 kHz**
The harsh S, T, and SH sounds in vocals and certain synth patches. Specific to the attack of transients in this range.
*Fix*: A narrow cut exactly at the sibilant peak (use the sweep technique). Or use a de-esser, which is a dynamic version of the same cut.

**Hiss / Noise Floor — 8 kHz and above**
Constant high-frequency noise from microphones, audio interfaces, or sample recordings. Not a resonance — a broadband noise floor.
*Fix*: A low-pass filter set just above the highest musically relevant frequency in the recording. Or a gentle high-shelf cut (−3 to −6 dB) above 10–12 kHz.

## How Much to Cut

The right amount of cut is the minimum that solves the problem. These are starting points:

| Problem Severity | Cut Amount | Q |
|---|---|---|
| Subtle resonance | −3 to −4 dB | 3–5 |
| Moderate resonance | −5 to −8 dB | 4–6 |
| Severe resonance | −8 to −12 dB | 5–8 |

Narrow Q for surgical work. Widen the Q slightly if you want the cut to affect a broader area.

## The Context Rule

Every surgical cut should be auditioned in the full mix, not in solo. A resonance that is extremely annoying in solo may be completely inaudible in the context of a full mix — cutting it wastes processing and potentially removes useful harmonic content. Conversely, a problem that seems subtle in solo may be causing serious masking in the mix.

The test: make the cut, toggle the EQ band off and on while the full mix plays. If you cannot hear a difference in the mix, the cut is doing nothing — remove it.
keyTakeaways: Surgical EQ always comes before tonal EQ — remove problems first, then shape character. Colouring a sound that still has a resonance just makes the resonance more prominent.
Use the sweep technique: narrow high-Q boost (+12 dB, Q of 8–12), drag slowly until the problem is exaggerated, then flip to a moderate cut (−4 to −8 dB) and widen the Q slightly.
Always A/B the cut in the full mix, not solo — if toggling the band on and off produces no audible difference in the mix context, the cut is doing nothing and should be removed.
lessons.tryThis: Pick the most problematic-sounding element in your current mix — the one that feels harsh, boxy, or muddy. Add Parametric EQ 2 and set a peak band to +12 dB with Q at 10. Play the track in a loop and drag the frequency slowly between 200 Hz and 3 kHz until the worst quality of the sound becomes grotesque. Note the exact frequency on the display. Switch the gain to −6 dB and set Q to 4. Now toggle the band on and off in the full mix — if you hear a clear improvement, keep the cut and adjust its depth. If you cannot hear a difference in context, remove the band entirely.

lessons.title: Tonal EQ: Shaping Character
lessons.body:
Once surgical cuts have removed the problems, tonal EQ shapes what remains into the sound you want. Where surgical EQ is about fixing, tonal EQ is about deciding — giving an element more warmth, more presence, more air, more weight. The tools are the same (Parametric EQ 2), but the mindset is different: you are painting, not erasing.

## The Tonal EQ Mindset

Tonal EQ uses broad, low-Q curves — shelves and gentle peaks — to change the overall character of a sound. Unlike the narrow surgical cuts that target specific resonances, tonal moves affect a wide swath of frequencies and are heard as a change in tone colour rather than the removal of a specific fault.

Key principle: **boost what you want to hear more of, not what is missing**. If a snare sounds thin, the instinct is to boost the low-mids to add body. But if the low-mids are already busy from other instruments, adding more just adds mud. The better move may be to boost the upper mids for snap, which makes the snare feel more present without adding low-end congestion.

## Low Shelf — Warmth and Weight

A low shelf boosts or cuts everything below a set frequency. Used with a moderate gain (±3 to ±6 dB) and a frequency between 100 and 250 Hz, it controls the warmth and weight of a sound.

**Boost**: Adds body and thickness. Useful on a thin kick that lacks presence on small speakers, or a bass that needs more warmth in the 150–200 Hz range.

**Cut**: Removes heaviness. Useful on pads and chords that feel too bottom-heavy even after high-passing — a gentle low-shelf cut at 200 Hz (−2 to −3 dB) lightens them without the sharp roll-off of a high-pass filter.

**Q on shelves**: Parametric EQ 2 lets you adjust the slope of a shelf. A steeper slope gives a more defined transition; a gentler slope sounds more natural. For musical tonal shaping, use moderate slope settings.

## High Shelf — Air and Brightness

A high shelf boosts or cuts everything above a set frequency. Set between 8 and 16 kHz with a gentle boost (+2 to +4 dB), it adds air, openness, and sparkle.

**Boost**: Makes a sound feel open and modern. The classic 'air boost' sits at 12–16 kHz, +2 to +3 dB. Used on leads, pads, and full mixes to add perceived space without affecting the midrange. Best with a wide, gentle slope.

**Cut**: Reduces brightness and harshness. A high-shelf cut at 10–12 kHz (−3 to −6 dB) tames digital-sounding synthesisers, overly bright samples, or cymbals that are too prominent.

## Peak Bell — Targeted Tonal Moves

For tonal work, use peak bells with a lower Q (0.5–2) than surgical EQ. Wide peaks affect a broad frequency region and sound musical — narrow peaks sound like corrections.

**Common tonal peak moves by instrument**:

*Kick drum*:
- +3 dB at 60–80 Hz (wide Q): sub punch and weight
- +2 dB at 3–5 kHz (moderate Q): click and attack on small speakers
- −3 dB at 200–300 Hz (moderate Q): remove boominess

*Snare*:
- +3 dB at 180–250 Hz (wide Q): add body and crack
- +3 dB at 5–8 kHz (moderate Q): crack and snap
- −3 dB at 400–600 Hz (moderate Q): remove cardboard tone

*Bass / 808*:
- +2 dB at 80–120 Hz (wide Q): reinforce the fundamental
- +3 dB at 700 Hz–1 kHz (moderate Q): add mid-bass presence for small speakers

*Lead synth / melody*:
- +2 dB at 3–5 kHz (wide Q): add presence and cut-through
- +3 dB at 8–12 kHz (wide Q): air and shimmer

*Pads / chords*:
- −3 dB at 300–500 Hz (moderate Q): remove muddiness
- +2 dB at 8–12 kHz (wide Q): add openness

## Boost Less Than You Think

The ear acclimates to boosts almost instantly. A +4 dB boost sounds impressive for 30 seconds, then becomes the new baseline — and the mix has lost 4 dB of headroom. Professional mixes routinely use boosts of +1.5 to +3 dB for tonal shaping.

The exception: corrective boosts compensating for a sample that genuinely lacks a frequency region. If a kick has virtually no sub energy, a +6 dB boost at 60 Hz may be appropriate. But verify it in context — boosts this large affect the mix balance and may require cuts elsewhere.

## Subtractive Tonal EQ

Frequently, the best way to make something sound brighter is to cut the lows and low-mids rather than boost the highs. The result is the same perceived brightness shift, but with less added energy, better headroom, and a cleaner mix. The question to ask before every boost: *can I achieve this by cutting somewhere else instead?*

## EQ Matching

Parametric EQ 2 does not have a built-in matching feature, but the principle is useful: if your lead sounds dull compared to a reference track, open the analyser on both (A/B through a reference track), compare the spectral shape, and make small tonal moves to bring yours closer. This is not about copying — it is about calibrating your ear to the difference.
keyTakeaways: Tonal EQ uses wide, low-Q curves (shelves and broad peaks) to change overall colour — use low shelves for warmth, high shelves for air, and gentle peak bells (Q 0.5–2) for targeted tonal character.
Before reaching for a boost, ask whether a cut elsewhere achieves the same perceived result — cutting lows to create apparent brightness keeps headroom and energy cleaner than boosting highs.

The ear adapts to boosts within seconds — professional tonal boosts are typically +1.5 to +3 dB. If you feel the urge to boost more than +4 dB, the source sound may need replacement rather than heavy EQ correction.
lessons.tryThis: On your lead synth or main melody, add Parametric EQ 2 after any surgical cuts. Set band 6 to a High Shelf with frequency at 12 kHz and gain at +2.5 dB — a classic air boost. Then set band 2 to a low-Q peak (Q = 0.8) at 3.5 kHz with +2 dB — a presence boost. Toggle the entire EQ on and off in the full mix. The lead should feel more open and forward without sounding brighter in an aggressive way. Now try pushing the high shelf to +6 dB and notice how quickly it becomes fatiguing — dial it back to the point where the improvement feels natural rather than obvious.

lessons.title: EQ for Frequency Separation
lessons.body:
Two instruments can each sound perfect in solo and still compete destructively in a mix. The reason is frequency overlap — they occupy the same range at similar levels, and the louder one masks the quieter one. EQ for frequency separation is the technique of giving each element its own dedicated frequency zone so all of them can be heard clearly at the same time.

## The Frequency Real Estate Concept

Think of the mix as a finite amount of frequency real estate. Each element needs space in that real estate to be heard. When two elements occupy the same space at the same level, neither is fully audible — they fight. When each element owns a distinct portion of the spectrum, all of them are clearly audible even at lower individual volumes.

The goal is not to isolate every element to a separate frequency island — that would sound unnatural. It is to ensure the most important parts of each element are not directly competing with the most important parts of another.

## Identifying Competing Elements

Competition happens most between elements in similar frequency ranges:

| Competing Pair | Overlap Zone |
|---|---|
| Kick + Bass / 808 | 60–120 Hz (sub and body) |
| Snare + Guitar / Chord pads | 200–500 Hz (body and low-mids) |
| Lead melody + Vocals | 1–4 kHz (presence and upper mids) |
| Hi-hats + Synth brightness | 6–12 kHz (upper presence and air) |
| Pad chords + Bass | 150–400 Hz (warmth and low-mids) |

## Complementary EQ: The Cut/Boost Pair

The most effective frequency separation technique works on pairs of competing elements simultaneously:

1. Identify where the two elements overlap
2. On element A (the one you want to win in that range): leave the frequency alone or boost it slightly
3. On element B (the one you want to pull back in that range): cut the same frequency

This is called **complementary EQ** — what one loses, the other gains. The result is both elements audible and distinct, occupying the same frequency space but at different levels within it.

**Kick and bass example**:
- Kick: boost 60–80 Hz for sub punch, cut 150–200 Hz slightly
- Bass: cut 60–80 Hz slightly (let the kick own the sub), boost 150–200 Hz for body

Result: the kick dominates the very low sub, the bass has body and warmth in the lower-mids, and both are clearly audible without fighting.

**Lead and pad example**:
- Pad: cut 2–4 kHz moderately (remove its presence range)
- Lead: boost 2–4 kHz slightly (reinforce its presence)

Result: the lead cuts through the pad without the pad needing to be turned down.

## High-Pass Filtering as Separation

The simplest and highest-impact separation tool is an aggressive high-pass filter on every element that does not need low-frequency energy. Every unnecessary sub and bass frequency from pads, leads, and percussion is clutter that competes with the bass and kick.

**Target high-pass frequencies by instrument type**:

| Instrument | Suggested High-Pass | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Chord pads | 200–300 Hz | Sub and bass belong to kick and 808 |
| Lead synth | 100–150 Hz | Clears the bass zone |
| Hi-hats / cymbals | 500–800 Hz | These are mid-high instruments |
| Atmospheric FX | 300–600 Hz | Remove low-end wash |
| Snare | 100–120 Hz | Sub belongs to kick |
| Clap | 150–200 Hz | Leave body, remove sub |

## Low-Pass Filtering as Separation

The same principle applies to the high end. If an 808 bass has unnecessary harmonic brightness above 3–4 kHz, it competes with synth leads in the presence range. A low-pass filter on the 808 above 3 kHz removes that competition.

**Target low-pass frequencies**:
- Bass / 808: low-pass at 3–5 kHz to remove presence competition
- Kick body samples: low-pass at 8–10 kHz if the sample has excessive hiss
- Room ambience: low-pass at 6–8 kHz to prevent competing with hi-hats

## The Mix-Context Approach

Frequency separation is always judged in the full mix, never in solo. The process:

1. Play the full mix
2. Identify the two elements that feel most blurred or competing
3. Solo both of them together (not individually) and use the spectrum analyser to find where they overlap
4. Apply complementary EQ cuts on the element you want to pull back in that range
5. Unsolo and listen in context
6. Repeat for the next competing pair

Work through competing pairs from the lowest frequency range upward — clear the sub first (kick vs bass), then the low-mids (snare vs pads), then the mids (lead vs chords), then the highs (hats vs synth air).
keyTakeaways: Complementary EQ — cutting one element's frequency range while leaving or boosting the same range on the competing element — is more effective than raising one element's volume to make it audible over another.

High-passing every non-bass element aggressively is the single highest-impact frequency separation move: every unnecessary low-frequency from pads, leads, and cymbals competes directly with kick and bass.
Always audition competing elements together (both soloed simultaneously), not individually — the overlap and masking between them is only audible when both are playing at once.
lessons.tryThis: Solo your bass and your chord pad simultaneously in the Mixer. Open the spectrum analyser on the chord pad and observe where both signals overlap — it is almost certainly in the 150–400 Hz range. Apply a high-pass filter on the pad at 250 Hz and a moderate cut (−3 dB) at 200 Hz. Then on the bass, add a gentle boost (+2 dB) at 180 Hz to fill the space the pad vacated. Unsolo and play the full mix. The bass should feel more present and defined without any volume change — you gave it frequency real estate that the pad was previously occupying.

lessons.title: Mid-Side EQ
lessons.body:
Standard EQ treats the left and right channels together. Mid-side EQ splits the stereo signal into two separate components — the **Mid** (what is identical in both channels, heard as the centre of the stereo field) and the **Side** (what differs between channels, heard as the width) — and lets you EQ them independently. This unlocks a level of spatial and tonal control that is impossible with normal stereo EQ.

## The Mid-Side Concept

Every stereo audio signal can be mathematically decomposed into:

**Mid channel** = Left + Right (summed, identical content between channels)
- Contains: kick, bass, lead vocal, main melody, centred elements
- In mono, you hear only the Mid

**Side channel** = Left − Right (the differences between channels)
- Contains: stereo width of pads, room reverb, panned layers, stereo effects
- Completely disappears in mono

EQ'ing the Mid affects centred elements. EQ'ing the Side affects only the stereo width information. This is a precise surgical tool.

## Enabling Mid-Side Mode in Parametric EQ 2

Parametric EQ 2 has a built-in mid-side mode:

1. Open Parametric EQ 2 on any stereo track or the master bus
2. Look for the **Stereo separation** toggle at the top of the plugin — it cycles between **Stereo** (normal), **Left**, **Right**, **Mid**, and **Side** modes
3. In **Mid** mode, all bands affect only the centre of the image
4. In **Side** mode, all bands affect only the stereo width information

For mid-side processing, you typically need two instances of Parametric EQ 2 in series — one set to Mid mode for mid processing, one set to Side mode for side processing. Or use a single instance and switch modes per band (FL Studio does not natively support per-band M/S within one instance, so two instances is the clean approach).

## Practical Mid-Side EQ Moves

### Low-pass or high-pass the Side channel
**Use case**: Keeping low frequencies mono. Bass and sub energy below about 150 Hz is non-directional — the human ear cannot localise low frequencies — but stereo low-end creates phase problems on mono playback and on subwoofers.

**Move**: On the Side channel instance, apply a high-pass filter at 100–150 Hz. This removes low-frequency side information, forcing bass and sub to be centred. The result is a tighter, cleaner low end that translates correctly to mono.

This is one of the most universally recommended master bus moves in professional mixing.

### High-shelf boost on the Side channel
**Use case**: Increasing perceived stereo width without touching the centre.

**Move**: On the Side channel, apply a high-shelf boost at 8–12 kHz of +2 to +4 dB. This brightens only the stereo content — the cymbals, the pad shimmer, the reverb tails — making the mix feel wider and more spacious without affecting the centred kick, bass, and lead.

### Low-mid cut on the Side channel
**Use case**: Cleaning up muddy width. Stereo low-mid content (200–400 Hz) is often phase-inconsistent and adds a blurry quality to the stereo image without contributing useful musical information.

**Move**: On the Side channel, cut −3 to −6 dB at 200–400 Hz with a moderate Q. The mix feels cleaner and more defined in width without losing stereo breadth.

### High-mid cut on the Mid channel
**Use case**: Reducing harshness from centred elements without affecting spatial information.

**Move**: On the Mid channel, apply a surgical or moderate cut at the harsh frequency (typically 2–5 kHz). This tames harshness from the kick, bass, and lead — the centred, most prominent elements — without dulling the shimmer and width of pads and reverbs in the Side channel.

## Mid-Side EQ on the Master Bus

The master bus is where mid-side EQ is most commonly applied, as a final mix-shaping tool:

1. **Side high-pass at 120 Hz**: Forces all sub and bass content to mono
2. **Side high-shelf boost at 10 kHz, +1.5 dB**: Opens the stereo image subtly
3. **Side low-mid cut at 300 Hz, −2 dB**: Cleans up blurry width in the low-mids
4. **Mid presence boost at 3 kHz, +1 dB**: Adds forward definition to centred elements

These four moves, used subtly, are common in professional mastering chains. On individual tracks, mid-side EQ is most useful on stereo synths, stereo pads, drum buses, and the master.

## When Not to Use Mid-Side EQ

Mid-side EQ on mono sources (a mono kick sample, a mono bass) has no effect on the Side channel — there is no side information to process. It is only useful on genuinely stereo signals. If you are unsure whether a source is stereo, check the Mixer meter — a true stereo signal shows slightly different levels on the L and R meters during playback.
keyTakeaways: A high-pass filter on the Side channel at 100–150 Hz forces bass and sub content to mono — it removes phase-cancelling low-frequency width information and is one of the most universally recommended master bus moves.
A high-shelf boost on the Side channel at 8–12 kHz widens the mix by brightening only the stereo content (pads, reverb, cymbal shimmer) without touching the centred kick, bass, and lead.
Mid-side EQ only affects stereo signals — applying it to mono sources has no effect on the Side channel because there is no stereo width information to process.
lessons.tryThis: Add two instances of Parametric EQ 2 to your master bus — label the first 'M' and the second 'S'. Set the first to Mid mode and the second to Side mode. On the Side instance, apply a high-pass filter at 120 Hz. Play your full mix through mono monitoring (use Fruity Stereo Enhancer at Width 0 on the master) and compare before and after the side high-pass — the low end should feel tighter and more defined in mono. Then switch back to stereo, add a high-shelf boost on the Side instance at 10 kHz of +2 dB, and hear how the mix opens up spatially without the centred elements changing character.

lessons.title: Dynamic EQ and Multiband Processing
lessons.body:
Standard EQ applies the same cut or boost regardless of how loud the signal is at any moment. Dynamic EQ and multiband compression change that — they only act when the signal in a specific frequency range exceeds a threshold. This makes them ideal for problems that are intermittent: a bass that gets muddy only on certain notes, a vocal that gets harsh only on loud phrases, a mix that gets congested only during the chorus.

## What Dynamic EQ Is

A dynamic EQ band behaves like a standard EQ band at low signal levels, but triggers a cut (or boost) when the signal in that band exceeds a threshold. Think of it as a frequency-specific compressor:

- **Threshold**: The level above which the dynamic cut/boost activates
- **Range**: How much the band moves when triggered (the maximum cut or boost)
- **Attack and release**: How quickly it reacts and recovers

Below the threshold, the signal passes through unaffected. Above it, the band applies its correction — only when needed, only at those frequencies.

## When to Use Dynamic EQ Instead of Static EQ

**Use static EQ** when a problem is consistent — a resonance that is always there at the same frequency and same level.

**Use dynamic EQ** when a problem is intermittent or level-dependent:
- A bass guitar that accumulates mud only when playing low notes
- A snare with a harsh peak that only becomes fatiguing on louder hits
- A mix that gets dense in the low-mids only during the full chorus
- A vocal that develops sibilance only on the loudest phrases

Dynamic EQ solves these problems without permanently cutting the frequency during the moments when it is not a problem.

## Parametric EQ 2 Does Not Have Dynamic EQ

FL Studio's stock Parametric EQ 2 is a static EQ — its bands do not respond to signal level. For dynamic EQ, you need either:

- **Fruity Peak Controller** feeding a static EQ's band gain (a workaround using automation)
- A third-party plugin (iZotope Neutron, TDR Nova, FabFilter Pro-Q 3 in dynamic mode)
- **Multiband compression** — a different but related tool that achieves similar results

## Multiband Compression

A multiband compressor splits the signal into frequency bands (typically 3–5) and compresses each band independently. This is similar to dynamic EQ but applies compression rather than EQ curve changes within each band.

**Fruity Multiband Compressor** (available in FL Studio) divides the signal into three bands:
- Low band (sub and bass frequencies)
- Mid band (mids and low-mids)
- High band (upper mids and highs)

Each band has its own threshold, ratio, attack, and release — exactly like a normal compressor, but operating only on that frequency range.

## Common Multiband Compression Uses

**Taming a bass that changes character across its range**:
A bass that sounds tight on high notes but boomy on low notes benefits from heavier low-band compression. Set the low band threshold low enough to catch the boomy low notes, while the mid and high bands use lighter settings. The result: a more even bass tone across its full range.

**Master bus multiband glue**:
A very light multiband compressor on the master (1–2 dB reduction per band, gentle ratios of 1.5:1 to 2:1) evens out the spectral balance of the mix during louder, denser sections. The low band catches sub energy spikes from 808 hits; the high band prevents harsh choruses. Use conservatively — over-multiband-compressing the master destroys transients and makes the mix sound processed.

**Drum bus control**:
Drums have energy across the full spectrum. A multiband compressor on the drum bus lets you control the low-end thump of the kick independently from the midrange snap of the snare and the high-frequency crack of the hats — all with one plugin.

## The Risk: Over-Processing

Dynamic EQ and multiband compression are powerful tools that are easy to overuse. Signs of over-processing:

- The mix sounds flat or lacks dynamics — everything feels compressed in an unmusical way
- Transients are dulled — the initial attack of drums and percussion is gone
- The mix sounds different (worse) when the plugin is bypassed in a way that is hard to explain
- Pumping or breathing artefacts that are not intentional

**Rule**: Use these tools to solve specific, identified problems. If you cannot name what problem you are solving, use a simpler tool. A well-gain-staged mix with good static EQ and standard compression rarely needs multiband processing on individual tracks — it is most valuable on buses and the master.

## A Simple Decision Tree

```
Is the problem always present at the same level?
  → Yes: Use static EQ
  → No: Is it level-dependent in one frequency band?
      → Yes: Use dynamic EQ
      → Is the whole mix getting too dense in one range?
          → Yes: Use multiband compression on the bus
```
keyTakeaways: Dynamic EQ is static EQ with a threshold — it only cuts or boosts when the signal in that frequency band exceeds a set level, making it ideal for intermittent problems that static EQ would over-correct.

Fruity Multiband Compressor lets you compress the low, mid, and high bands of a signal independently — use it on bass to even out character across note ranges, or lightly on the master bus to control spectral balance during dense sections.

If you cannot name the specific problem dynamic EQ or multiband compression is solving, use a simpler tool — these are problem-solvers, not default inserts, and overuse destroys transients and mix dynamics.
lessons.tryThis: Open Fruity Multiband Compressor on your drum bus. Set all three bands to ratio 2:1 and pull each threshold down until you see 2–3 dB of gain reduction on the loudest hits. Set attack to 10 ms and release to 150 ms on all bands. Toggle the plugin on and off — the drums should feel slightly more even and controlled with it on, without losing punch or transient impact. Now push the ratio to 6:1 and lower the threshold further — hear how the over-compressed version sounds flat and lifeless compared to the subtle version. Dial back to the setting where compression is felt as cohesion rather than heard as squashing.
